design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate side-scrolling levels using gun and whip mechanics, defeat boss encounters, progress through themed stages with occasional gameplay variations
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing dual attack mechanics (gun vs. whip) while navigating hazards and enemies; protecting NPC allies in specific levels creates secondary tension
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as an unlikely hero—a worm in a robotic suit—engaging in absurdist action-platforming with quirky, surreal antagonists
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberate tonal irreverence and satire of platform game conventions; created by TenNapel as satire after years of licensed game restrictions. Refuses conventional heroic framing in favor of surreal humor, bizarre bosses, and unexpected level variations (racing, bungee-jumping, underwater mazes). The game embraces stylistic variety over mechanical consistency.

difficulty.lives_system: Health-based system with depletion from enemy contact and hazards; Peter Puppy protection mechanic can trigger rapid health loss if he takes damage
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based progression; levels completed sequentially with boss gates
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Some levels introduce mandatory protection mechanics (e.g., 'For Pete's Sake' requires keeping Peter Puppy safe), removing traditional player autonomy. Racing sequences ('Andy Asteroids') gate progression—player must beat Psy-Crow or fight him as boss; this creates variable difficulty paths.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Boss defeats unlock next level; racing segments ('Andy Asteroids') act as gates between main stages
replayability_hooks: Multiple platform ports with different technical implementations (Genesis vs. SNES versions differ)
Varied level objectives beyond stage completion (protecting NPCs, time limits, avoidance challenges)
Competitive racing segments create variable outcomes

world_building_techniques: Character-driven antagonists with distinctive designs and naming (Queen Slug-for-a-Butt, Evil the Cat, Bob the Killer Goldfish, Professor Monkey-for-a-Head)
Absurdist premise: earthworm gains agency through suit acquisition; motivations are comical (pursuing Princess What's-Her-Name)
Environmental variety reflects character archetypes (volcanic lairs, jungles, technological spaces)
Dark humor subtext: princess crushed by cow Jim launched into space; Peter Puppy's violent outbursts when unprotected

boss_design.philosophy: Diverse, visually distinctive antagonists with surreal or grotesque designs; each boss occupies unique thematic space rather than mechanical archetype repetition
notable_bosses: Psy-Crow (recurring, appears in racing segments and boss fights)
Robotic Chicken (exclusive to Professor Monkey fight)

faction_indicators: Princess What's-Her-Name represents aspirational goal (though subverted by cow crushing)
Peter Puppy acts as moral anchor—protecting him creates ally investment
Psy-Crow serves as recurring rival across racing and combat encounters
danger_signals: Queen Slug-for-a-Butt embodies grotesque, body-horror threat
Junkyard setting (Chuck's domain) suggests civilizational decay
Rapid environmental shifts (volcanic, underwater, technological) signal unpredictability
environmental_storytelling: Level progression through themed environments (jungles, cities, space) establishes escalating stakes
Peter Puppy as environmental character requiring active protection creates implicit narrative about vulnerability
Boss lair designs reflect character traits (grotesque antagonists occupy surreal spaces)
